title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption:
Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements

Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.

The line chart tracks the cumulative count of global parties joining twelve specific multilateral environmental agreements between 1971 and 2015. The vertical axis measures the number of parties from 0 to 200, while the horizontal axis spans the years. The Vienna Convention on Ozone, represented by the dark blue line, begins its ascent in 1971 and reaches the highest point on the chart, approaching 200 parties by 2015. The UN Framework Convention on Climate Change and the Convention on Biological Diversity follow similar trajectories, starting in the early 1990s and rising steeply to join the top cluster near the 200 mark. The UN Convention to Combat Desertification and the Kyoto Protocol also show rapid growth after their inception in the 1990s, ending in the 150 to 190 range. In the middle of the distribution, the World Heritage Convention and the Cites Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora display steady, continuous growth starting from the 1970s, finishing between 150 and 175 parties. The Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants and the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety remain flat at zero until the late 1990s or early 2000s before climbing to roughly 170 and 160 respectively. The Ramsar Convention and the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea show earlier starts but slower accumulation, ending near 170 and 165. The Rotterdam Convention and the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals occupy the lowest positions, with the latter starting in 1979 and reaching only about 125 parties by 2015. The data shows a consistent pattern where newer agreements tend to accumulate signatories more rapidly in their initial decades compared to older treaties.
